sentences.text: My name is Olga Drucker.
O-L-G-A D-R-U-C-K-E-R.
Olga Levy.
O-L-G-A.
And I have a middle name, but I-- Maximiliane-- And L-E-V-Y.
Well, my parents changed the family name when I was about two years old, something like that, to Lenk, L-E-N-K.
I think it was for political reasons and also mainly for business reasons tied in together with that.
I was born in December of 1927.
In Stuttgart, Germany.
S-T-U-T-T-G-A-R-T.
He was a book publisher.
Together with my uncle, his brother, they owned the--
Yeah, they published children's books.
And the whole business was founded by my grandfather, their father.
So this was an old, established business.
Since 1872 or 1871.
I forget.
He was born in Stuttgart also in October, October 11, 1880.
Other than his own love for language-- he was a doctor of philology-- he collected stamps, and he liked to hike.
He played music.
He played the piano.
Our house was always full of music.
No.
He died in '70-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I forgot.
He was almost 93 years old, so I guess it would have been '73.
In the United States, yes.
Her name was Martha, Martha Lenk.
She was Martha Levy when she married my father.
And her maiden name was Hirsch.
H-I-R-S-C-H.
She was born in Wiesbaden on September 24, 1891.
Well, yeah, she loved to read.
She sewed.
Also, she was musical.
She used to sing in a chorus.
She-- I think she just liked the arts.
No.
She died in 1967 of cancer in the United States.
I have a brother.
He's my only brother, my only sibling.
He's older than I am.
His name is Herbert Lenk.
In Germany, his name was Hans, H-A-N-S, Hans Herbert.
And when he became an American, he switched it around to Herbert Hans.
He's now in Florida.
He and his wife lived in, in Cincinnati for many years.
Caesar Fleischlenstrasse funf-- five.
[LAUGHS]
C-A-E-S-A-R, Caesar.
Fleischlen is F-L-E-I-S-C-H-L-E-N, and strasse means street.
S-- you know.
Oh, yeah.
It was-- well, this was a house that my father had built, and my uncle had a similar house right next door.
And they were Bauhaus-style houses.
They were square with flat roofs, stucco, three floors with verandas and big windows and a nice garden for me to play in.
I also could play on, on the roof because it was flat with a fence around it.
Besides my parents and my brother?
Not of the family.
But we had a-- we had a cook, and we had a, a maid, the woman that pretty much brought me up, actually.
And her name was Frieda.
I'm not sure what her last name was.
Well, Jewish life, I'm not sure.
It was just life for me, because I wasn't really taught anything about being Jewish.
I, I, other-- I just knew that we were Jewish.
When-- from what time on I knew that I, I really don't remember.
But we didn't have much of a Jewish life at all.
I remember one time going to a synagogue with my uncle.
Perhaps it was a holiday, I don't know.
No, they didn't.
And as a matter of fact, we did have, as did quite a few other German Jewish families, I think we had a Christmas tree, ostensibly for the maid, but--
--we enjoyed it, too.
Yeah, yeah.
German.
No, no.
Probably.
I don't know.
I think most of their friends and acquaintances-- well, friends, let's say, must have been Jewish, I think.
[LAUGHS]
Yeah, both.
Both from school, yeah.
I started going to school quite late actually because I was very ill when I was a little girl.
I had a middle ear infection.
And my mother-- my mother didn't want me to-- she thought I was delicate, which I'm far from, never was.
But anyway, she held me back for a year so that by the time I did get to school, I was able to read and write already.
I was--
--seven years old.
Yeah.
Yeah.
This was a private girls' school.
And, and I had a teacher who-- in Germany I don't know if it's still the same.
The teachers move up with their class.
They don't get a new teacher every time they move up.
So Fraulein [NON-ENGLISH] was my teacher, and I loved her.
I would have to count them.
I have a picture of them.
But I'd say about between 15 and 20 perhaps.
Well, we certainly learned to write.
You know, we had composition.
We had arithmetic, of course.
And we must have had some history and geography.
I know we had music.
We  all learned the recorder, played a recorder.
We had gymnastics.
And that was it.
Then I think there was some-- I'm going to say something I'm not sure about, that I know that the children had to have religious instruction, and I'm not sure what I did.
I can't remember.
No.
They probably were, so-- but I don't remember getting any Jewish instruction until later on when I had to.
But that comes later in the story.
Again, there wasn't much of that.
It was-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] let me think.
I have to think about that.
There was one.
This was after Kristallnacht already.
One of my friends, a non-Jewish friend, met me coming towards her in the street.
And when-- and I had already-- I wasn't going to that school anymore.
I was by then going to a Jewish school.
And she crossed the street without waving to me or without recognizing me, and I was devastated.
And I guess you could call that an anti-Semitic incident because that's when my mother explained to me that I was Jewish.
Very little, except that it was not good.
It wasn't something good, because everybody was against us, you know.
And we were supposed to be so, such terrible people, and I never had anything very positive about that.
Yes.
Yeah, that I remember vividly.
Well, November 9, the night of November 9 and 10 in 1938.
And I remember waking up because there were-- I heard some people coming into the house in the middle of the night--
--and coming up the steps.
I was almost 11.
I was still 10 years old.
And I was very scared.
I didn't know who was in this house and why they were making such a racket and banging doors.
And, I, I heard my mother crying at one point.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And then everything got quiet, and she came into my room.
And I, I didn't-- I couldn't face it, I guess.
Denial is sometimes my middle name.
And so I pretended to be asleep, and she went out again.
But that was the night that they came for my father, as they came for all males-- we learned later-- between 16 and 60 or 80.
I don't know what the cut-off date was.
And, and then my mother got on the phone and tried to find out where they took him.
And she called the police station, and they had taken, taken him there.
My uncle next door was there, too.
And from there, they had taken him to Dachau.
Yeah.
I think so, yeah.
I don't know.
Well, no.
Well, perhaps she did know.
I, I-- at that time, I knew it was a concentration camp.
No, I don't think so.
No, no, we never really did.
Not to my old school, no.
It was a-- I guess a few days went by, and then the-- and then I was sent to a Jewish school.
These Jewish schools were open, schools were opened up immediately so that the children could go on going to school.
The Jewish community.
The Jewish community was well organized.
I guess, yeah.
All I remember-- and it was only a few months that I was going there anyway.
What I remember, it was November, so Hanukkah was coming up, and I learned about Hanukkah.
I never knew.
And when I came home to tell my mother about it-- because at that point, everybody was gone.
My, my maid, Frieda, was no longer with us because she wasn't-- I'm getting to Hanukkah-- because she, she wasn't allowed by, by the Nuremberg laws to work for us anymore.
Rationale?
[LAUGHS]
Oh, well, any young, so-called Aryan woman under a certain age-- let's make it 35.
I'm not, I'm not positive about that-- couldn't work for Jewish people anymore probably-- I don't know, the Jewish people were going to do something dreadful to them.
Anyway.
And, and my brother was already in England in school at that time, and now my father was gone.
So it was just my mother and I.
And I came home and told her about Hanukkah.
And she took me by the hand, and we went up to the third floor, where there was like a storage room, and she found this, this Hanukkah menorah.
And she must have-- I mean, she must have known that it was there all along.
But she brought that out, and we actually had a Hanukkah.
I had a sheet with the songs on it and--
My first Hanukkah.
We lit the candles.
Yep.
He did return after, I think, it was seven or eight weeks.
Well, he came home on my birthday, which is on the 28th of December.
And-- but he wasn't allowed to talk about Dachau, and he never did.
Yeah.
He was very thin and very old-looking, and he had frostbite on his toes.
And it was bad, but it was good to have him back home again.
Yeah.
I, I think-- he was never the most cheerful of men, I mean, but he would crack a joke every now and then.
But after that, it was like everything was dead serious, you know.
Everything was so serious.
And of course, I don't know if you want me to talk about this.
At this point, when my father was already in Dachau, my mother made the decision to have my name placed on the Kindertransport list.
OK.
It was from that night.
Everything stems back to that night.
Kristallnacht, yes.
Excuse me.
[DRINKS] And she heard about this-- this, this was an organized plan to rescue as many children-- rescue-- to get children out of Germany.
At that point, everybody knew that it was not good to stay there anymore.
So the thing was to get the children out as fast as possible.
So this was organized by the Jewish community throughout Germany and Austria, Czechoslovakia and Poland as well, a few in Poland.
And also not only Jewish, but Christian.
A lot of Quakers were involved with it.
And we were going to be taken to England.
And this was a, a tremendous undertaking.
In England, they had to pass an act of Parliament to get the funding to-- and, and to get people to take us in.
It was just--
There were private families that would take us.
There were institutions that would take us.
Some of them were very Orthodox Jewish organizations.
Others were-- it was the whole spectrum.
It didn't have to be Jewish at all.

As a matter of fact, I-- after my first-- I'm jumping the gun-- but after my first experience with a Jewish family, I, I was living with Christian people all along-- the rest of the time that I was in England.
Yeah, I know.
I'm jumping ahead.
OK.
She, she had to just put my name on a list.
Whether she paid any money for this, I don't know.
She-- I know that that wasn't the requirement.
You didn't have to pay for it.
But since we were well off, I, I wouldn't be surprised if she did contribute to it.
And then, she-- of course, she had to prepare me.
You know, I was a little girl, and she had to--
--get me used to it. 11.
Well, she got a teacher in to teach me some English, and that's number one, I guess.
And I learned to say, the dog is under the table.
[LAUGHS]
Very.
Also, there were things to pack, what I could take.
Too much, actually.
Most of the stuff I never saw anymore.

But clothing, and she actually packed up what amounts to a trousseau, I think, you know, bed sheets and that sort of thing, which, to this day, I know I don't have them anymore, but I did have for a long time.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And then this committee, the Refugee Committee, it was called, sent us the name of the family that I was to be with.
Yes.
And that family sent us some letters and a picture of themselves.
Right.
Well, I-- I didn't actually go till March, so from November to March.
I was still going to the Jewish school.
Then I learned about Purim and Haman.
And I said Haman-- I told my mother that Haman's just like Hitler.
And she went pale because you're not allowed to say things, not even in your own house, you know.
The saying used to be   that the walls had ears, and they might well have.
I don't know.
They just went on going to school because they weren't Jewish.
And the Jewish schoolmates, I don't even remember them anymore.
I think I must have blocked a lot of stuff, and also it was such a short time from November to March that I--
Well, my parents and I went in the business car to the railroad station.
Somebody else drove us because neither of my parents drove.
Your father was still in business at that time.
Yes.
Well, no.
Not really, no.
The business had been taken from him a while.
Since 1936 he had been forced to sell the business.
This is now 1930-- early 1939.
So he was really in a-- but we still had a car available.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Some things don't make any sense as I tell them, but this is how I remember it.
Anyway, we went to the station, and my mother was allowed to come on the train with me as far as Wiesbaden.
That's about, I'd say, about an hour on the train.
It was early morning.
Well, my cousin was with me, my cousin Hilda, who's-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] who lived next door.
So you had a pretty close relationship with her.
Yeah.
She's three years older, so she was 14 at the time.
Not really.
I don't think so.
I don't know.
I seem to remember that I was pretty alone.
Well, my mother was still with me for a little while anyway.
Oh, yeah.
[LAUGHS] Isn't it funny the things you remember?
I had on a blue woolen dress.
And it was wool, and I-- to this day, I'm allergic to wool.
But who knew from allergic in those days, right?
So I wore this blue dress, and I had underpants to match.
And I remember an incident on that train where I had to go to the bathroom.
And when I came out of it, everybody was pointing at me and laughing.
And I looked down, and I had my dress caught in my underpants.
[LAUGHS]
Yes.
I remember it, like, 60 years late-- no, not-- 50 years later.
[LAUGHS]
Well, we didn't have the whole train.
It was not a special train.
It was a regular passenger train, and we might have had one or two cars.
I can't say for sure, but there might have been about 100 kids on that train.
And we were all very excited, of course.
And the kids were like from very, very young, three, four years old, to-- the cut-off age was 17.
So it was all a mixture of kids.
And of course, we all had to-- wanted to be by the window, you know.
And I know I, I was able to wave good-bye to my father in Stuttgart.
They said they would come to England in about six weeks after, and I believed them.
[INAUDIBLE].
I think not too many, except for the German officers that were on the train with us, you know, they were the SS people.
I don't know.
To keep us in line or to make sure that we were going where we were going, and-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I don't know.
I have-- I don't remember anything bad happening on my particular train.
But I've heard other children who had come out on other Kindertransports, where these SS people were really nasty and opened up their suitcases and just threw everything around and really mean.
But I honestly don't remember anything like that happening.
Well, then my-- this was the town that my mother was born in and where my grandmother still lived, and she came to the train station to say good-bye to me.
Again, I don't think I understood the, the gravity of it.
I don't think I understood that perhaps this would be the last time we would see each other.
And indeed, it was the last time I would see my grandmother because later she was killed in Theresienstadt.
But she brought me a little present.
Yeah.
It, it isn't the original, but it was exactly like that.
Where should I hold it, like that?
It was-- it's-- actually, there were two books.
One was this color, and the other one was red.
And this one's from German to English, and the other one was from English to German.
And this is how I got by the first few weeks that I was in England, because just to be able to say that the dog's under the table doesn't help you very much.
And so if I wanted to communicate, I used the dictionaries and the other way around.
OK?
Well, my mother got off in Wiesbaden, and [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] then I was on my own on the train.
And we got to-- I guess it must have taken pretty much a full day when we finally arrived in the Hook of Holland in Holland, which is where we were to get on the boat to go to England.
When we got to the border of Germany and Holland, the German officials got off, and the Dutch officials came on.
And I guess we had to-- there must have been some other adults with us because they carried our papers, our passports.
We all had numbers hanging around our necks and little cardboard things.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And of course, the Dutch officials were much more human, even human-looking, you know.
Are you OK?
I'm fine.
What else?
I don't know if we ate.
We must have had something on the train.
I don't remember.
I know that we were greeted with stuff in Holland.
There were some women, Dutch women, there who were ready for us with--
Well, it was hot chocolate and cookies and stuff.
It wasn't-- it wasn't a full meal.
I don't think it was even sandwiches.
But it was something.
And then we were taken onto the ferry boats.
And this was already evening.
It was an overnight [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] journey.
And I was-- I had lost my cousin by then.
We didn't see each other anymore.
She must have been on that boat, too, but we weren't kept together.
And I was put on a, on a, in a room with a-- a cabin with an older girl, and she cried all the way over.
And so that's when I started crying, too.
